authors: Kris Shengjun Dong, Sahil Modi, Dima Nikiforov, Sana Damani, Edward Lin, Siva Kumar Sastry Hari, Christos Kozyrakis
title: KernelBlaster: Continual Cross-Task CUDA Optimization via Memory-Augmented In-Context Reinforcement Learning
storage_format: Persistent CUDA Knowledge Base (~50KB JSON): hierarchy of performance states (memory-bound, compute-bound, hybrid) → lists of candidate optimizations with expected performance scores. Each entry ⟨state, (optimization, score)⟩. Updated via PolicyEvaluation (compares result vs prediction), PerfGapAnalysis (analyzes discrepancy), ParameterUpdate (rewrites the KB).
benchmarks_used: KernelBench Level 1, Level 2, Level 3 (NVIDIA A6000, H100, L40S GPUs)
performance_metrics: Geometric mean speedup, fast_p (% kernels with ≥r× speedup), ValidRate
baseline_compared: AI CUDA Engineer, IREE compiler, PyTorch (Eager + torch.compile), Kernelsseum (zero-shot), no_mem_agent (ablation)
memory_size_over_time: ~50KB Knowledge Base; grows with discovered states (~5.5 average states per kernel); accumulated optimizations ~60 unique after 8000 attempts
token_reduction: 2.4× fewer tokens than the minimal agent without a Knowledge Base
main_result: KernelBlaster achieves geometric mean speedups of 1.43×, 2.50× and 1.50× on KernelBench Level 1-3 vs PyTorch. The persistent Knowledge Base enables cross-task and cross-GPU transfer, with a 1.67× gain over the memoryless agent.
performance_vs_baseline: GeoMean 1.43× (L1), 2.50× (L2), 1.50× (L3) vs PyTorch; 1.67× vs no_mem_agent; surpasses AI CUDA Engineer in fast_p for moderate to high speedups
cost_vs_baseline: 2.4× fewer tokens than the minimal agent without KB; KB reusable across GPUs (~50KB compact)
limitations: Evaluation limited to NVIDIA CUDA kernels; Level 3 has scaling challenges with single optimization per iteration; the Knowledge Base may be biased toward early entries; dependence on detailed NCU profiling for quality feedback
validity_threats: KernelBench as the only benchmark; specific NVIDIA GPUs (Ampere, Hopper, Ada); reward hacking possible even with correctness verification; comparison with AI CUDA Engineer under different evaluation conditions (models, hardware, test harness)
